Home

Iran Nuclear Deal Talks Resume in Vienna

By John Smith, Diplomatic Correspondent
May 5, 2026

World powers and Iran resumed negotiations in Vienna on Monday, marking the first formal talks since the breakdown of discussions last year.

Delegates from the E3 (France, Germany, UK), China, Russia, and Iran gathered at the Palais Coburg hotel for what officials described as "preliminary discussions" aimed at reviving the 2015 nuclear agreement.

"All parties have shown willingness to engage constructively," said EU foreign policy chief Enrique Mora, who is chairing the talks. "There is a clear understanding that time is of the essence."

The Biden administration has signaled its readiness to rejoin the agreement if Iran returns to full compliance. However, significant obstacles remain, including Iran's demand for sanctions relief and verification mechanisms.

A senior Western diplomat told reporters that the initial session focused on sequencing — which steps each side would take first. "The atmosphere was professional, but nobody is expecting a breakthrough this week," the diplomat said on condition of anonymity.

Iran's chief negotiator, Ali Bagheri Kani, reiterated Tehran's position that all sanctions imposed since 2017 must be lifted before Iran reverses its nuclear steps. Western powers countered that some sanctions are unrelated to the nuclear deal.

The talks come amid rising concerns over Iran's uranium enrichment levels, which now stand at 60% — far beyond the 3.67% limit set by the JCPOA. International Atomic Energy Agency inspectors recently reported that Iran's stockpile of enriched uranium has reached unprecedented levels.

Oil markets reacted cautiously to the news, with Brent crude dipping 1.2% on hopes that a deal could eventually bring Iranian oil back to global markets.
